summary.source: Draft SReq (Feb 2026) — 4 standards, all listed individually

info:
About this family: Covers factory-made precast concrete elements such as hollow core slabs, beams, wall and facade panels, stairs, masts, bridge elements, and box culverts — in normal-weight, lightweight aggregate and autoclaved aerated concrete. Products range from simple unreinforced items to prestressed structural members. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 229 (precast concrete products), with CEN/TC 177 contributing standards for lightweight aggregate and autoclaved aerated concrete products.

Current harmonised standards: EN 1520:2011 (lightweight aggregate, AVCP System 2+), EN 12602:2016 (autoclaved aerated concrete, AVCP System 2+). Both cited in OJ under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/100 (1994). EN 13369:2023 (common rules; national adoptions 2024) replacing EN 13369:2018 — this is a non-harmonised reference standard (no Annex ZA), but underpins all precast product standards. AVCP per Commission Decision 1999/94/EC (System 2+ for structural, System 4 for non-structural).

Standards in development: prEN 12602 — draft standard dated 24 July 2025 (BSI reference 25/30510163 DC), confirming revision already underway under CEN/TC 177. EN 1170:2024 (test methods for fibre-reinforced precast) published Dec 2024 — supporting standard development is active.

Standardisation request: Acquis Milestone I (product scope) and Milestone III (essential characteristics) both completed. Draft implementing decision published 4 Feb 2026 via Art. 12 notification (earlier draft from April 2024). Feedback until 3 Mar 2026. Under CPR 305/2011 (old regulation). Replaces mandate M/100 (1994). 4 deliverables, all due 30 Oct 2026: (1) new hEN for precast concrete performance assessment (Table 1), (2) EPD product category rules for lightweight and AAC products (Table 1), (3) revision of EN 1520:2011 (Table 2), (4) revision of EN 12602:2016 (Table 2). Decision expires 30 Jun 2027. CEN reports due 31 Jan 2027. Table 3 target of SReq Q2 2025 was missed by ~8 months. NOT yet adopted — CEN WP 2025 still lists as "M/XXX" (no mandate number assigned).

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2031, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request draft published (Feb 2026) and delivery expected Q4 2026, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2030–2031. Note: this standardisation request is under CPR 305/2011 – a subsequent request under CPR 2024/3110 is needed before DPP applies. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 4 hENs range from ~2030 to ~2031 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: SReq C(2025) 6586 + EOTA PA 20. 12 hENs + 28 EADs.

info:
About this family: Covers structural steel and aluminium products used in load-bearing construction, including hot-rolled and cold-formed sections, stainless steel structural members, high-strength and non-preloaded bolting assemblies, welding consumables, and insulating sandwich panels. Conformity assessment centres on the execution of steel and aluminium structures and their components. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 135.

Current harmonised standards: EN 1090-1:2009+A1:2011 is the primary conformity assessment standard, but the OJ-cited harmonised standards for this family also include EN 15088:2005, EN 10340:2007, EN 14399-1:2015, EN 15048-1:2007, EN 13479:2017, EN 10025-1:2004, EN 10210-1:2006, EN 10219-1:2006, EN 10088-4:2009, and EN 10088-5:2009 (all under mandate M/120 and CPR 305/2011). Supported by execution standards EN 1090-2:2018+A1:2024 (steel, sustainability provisions added), EN 1090-3:2019 (aluminium), EN 1090-4:2018 and EN 1090-5:2017 (cold-formed). CEN/TS 1090-201:2024 covers structural steel reuse.

EN 1090-1 mandate rejection (summer 2025): The European standardisation mandate for the EN 1090-1 revision was rejected by a majority of EU member states. Concerns raised: unclear scope, undefined terminology, unnecessary/missing performance characteristics (welding suitability, fracture toughness, fatigue strength removed). CEN development stage dropped from 40.60 to 10.99. A revised mandate is expected early 2026. Publication now estimated ~2028–2029 at earliest.

Standards in development (per TC 135 work programme):
• prEN 1090-1 rev — mandate rejected, back to stage 10.99
• prEN 1090-4 rev — cold-formed steel (stage 40.60, vote May 2026, NOT harmonised under CPR)
• prEN 1090-5 rev — cold-formed aluminium (stage 40.60, vote Jul 2026, NOT harmonised under CPR)
• prEN 17662 — EPD PCR for steel/iron/aluminium (stage 45.99, FV dispatched Dec 2023 — possibly stalled)

28+ EADs under Product Area 20: Structural steel sections (EAD 200017, 200022, 200116), tension rods/cables (EAD 200001, 200002, 200006, 200032), piles/foundations (EAD 200005, 200014, 200036, 200043, 200077), gabions/baskets (EAD 200019, 200020, 200026, 200039), connections (EAD 200033, 200086, 200102), corrosion protection (EAD 200126, 200188), and others. Previously reported as 0 EADs.

Standardisation request: Acquis Milestone I (product scope) and Milestone III (essential characteristics) both completed. Commission Implementing Decision C(2025) 6586, adopted 2 October 2025 under CPR 305/2011 (replacing mandate M/120). 12 standards (5 new, 7 revisions), all due 15 November 2025 — deadline missed (~6 weeks from adoption). Decision expires 31 Jan 2027. COM(2025) 772 confirms SMP as first-wave priority for CPR 2024/3110. New SReq under new CPR planned Q2 2026 (delivery Q3 2027).

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request adopted (Oct 2025) and delivery expected 15 Nov 2025 (overdue), the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2031–2032. Note: this standardisation request is under CPR 305/2011 – a subsequent request under CPR 2024/3110 is needed before DPP applies. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 12 hENs and 28 EADs range from ~2030 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2030–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2031–2032).

summary.source: SReq C(2025) 4828 Annex I + EOTA database

info:
About this family: Covers the full range of cements, building limes, and hydraulic binders used in construction, from common Portland cements and masonry cements to specialist types such as calcium aluminate, supersulphated, and very low heat cements, as well as hydraulic road binders. Cement is explicitly carved out of the ESPR, meaning sustainability requirements are set under the CPR instead. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 51.

Current harmonised standards: EN 197-1:2011 (common cement), EN 14216:2015, EN 413-1:2011, EN 14647:2005, EN 15743:2010+A1:2015, EN 459-1:2010, EN 13282-1:2013, EN 15368:2008+A1:2010. All cited under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/114 (1997). GWP declarations mandatory from 8 Jan 2026 (Annex II). CBAM financial obligations from 1 Jan 2026.

Standards in development: prEN 197-1 rev — new project approved 2 July 2025 by CEN/TC 51 (stage 10.99). Revision will integrate EN 197-5 (CEM II/C-M and CEM VI low-carbon cements) and EN 197-6 (cement with recycled building materials), enabling wider range of low-carbon cements. EN 196-12:2024 published Dec 2024 (reactivity test methods supporting performance-based approach). NOTE: Only EN 197-1 has a registered CEN prEN project. All other 8 standards covered by C(2025) 4828 have no registered prEN yet — risk for 2027-2028 deadlines.

Standardisation request: Acquis Milestone I (product scope) and Milestone III (essential characteristics) both completed. Commission Implementing Decision C(2025) 4828, adopted 28 July 2025. Confirmed under CPR 2024/3110 (title: "in support of Regulation (EU) 2024/3110"). DPP applies directly. Replaces mandate M/114 (expired 31 Dec 2025). 10 deliverables — 1 new TR + 9 revisions (Annex I). Deadlines: EN 197-1 + EN 16908 by 30 Jun 2027; EN 413-1, EN 459-1, EN 15368, alkali-activated TR by 31 Dec 2027; EN 14216, EN 14647, EN 15743, EN 13282 by 30 Jun 2028. Decision expires 30 Jun 2030.

EAD route: EAD 150080-00-0301 (blended cements with reduced clinker content) formally cited in OJEU December 2025 — milestone for low-carbon cement technologies. Ecocem holds ETA 23-0877 (ACT technology, issued by Cerema Dec 2023) and is building a EUR 50M production facility in Dunkirk (300kt/yr capacity, commercial operation H2 2026). Commission signalled Feb 2025 that an EOTA mandate for alkali-activated cements EAD could follow by 2027.

Alkali-activated cements: Downgraded to Technical Report in C(2025) 4828, despite strong stakeholder opposition (19 signatories). CEN/TC 51 refused to develop standards. A performance-based standard for alternative low-carbon cements requested for delivery by 2030.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2029–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request adopted (Jul 2025) and delivery expected Jun 2027–Jun 2028, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2029–2030. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 9 hENs and 1 EAD range from ~2029 to ~2032 (8 of 9 hENs under CPR 305/2011, needing revision under CPR 2024/3110). The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2029–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2029–2030).

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 166 + EOTA PA 6. 18 hENs + 11 EADs.

info:
About this family: Covers system chimneys and their components, including metal, clay/ceramic and concrete chimney liners, flue pipes, flue blocks, outer wall elements, flue terminals, chimney fans, draught regulators, air/flue terminal systems for room-sealed appliances, and chimney kits (via EADs). Materials span metal, clay/ceramic, concrete and plastic. Free-standing industrial chimneys are excluded and follow a separate standardisation track. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 166 (hENs) and EOTA (EADs).

Current harmonised standards: 18 harmonised product standards are cited in the OJ under mandate M/105 (CPR 305/2011), with AVCP System 2+ as the primary system for general characteristics (reaction to fire assessed separately under System 1 or 3 per Commission Decision 95/467/EC as amended by 2010/679/EU). Key standards include: EN 1856-1:2009 and EN 1856-2:2009 (metal chimneys), EN 1857:2010 and EN 1858:2008+A1:2011 (concrete components), EN 1457-1:2012 and EN 1457-2:2012 (clay/ceramic flue liners), EN 13063-1/2/3 (clay/ceramic system chimneys), EN 14471:2013+A1:2015 (plastic flue liners), and EN 14989-1/2:2007 (room-sealed applications). EN 1443:2019 serves as the general requirements reference standard.

European Assessment Documents: 11 chimney-specific EADs exist under Product Area 06, of which 10 are cited in the OJ (7 via Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/450 as amended, 3 via earlier C-series OJ communications). All under AVCP System 1. EAD 060017 is pending OJ citation. These are old-regime EADs under CPR 305/2011 — they can be used for issuing ETAs until 9 January 2031, and existing ETAs remain valid for CE marking until 9 January 2036. DPP does not apply to products covered by these old-regime EADs.

Standards in development: CEN/TC 166 is proactively revising several standards under its own work programme. These revisions are TC-driven under the existing mandate M/105 (CPR 305/2011) — they are not yet mandated under CPR 2024/3110 and do not trigger DPP obligations on their own. However, the revision work is expected to be incorporated into the forthcoming standardisation request under CPR 2024/3110.
• prEN 1856-1 rev — metal system chimney products (revision status uncertain — genorma shows CEN project deleted Mar 2021, but DIN/BSI references suggest a draft was circulated and may have restarted)
• prEN 1856-2 rev — metal flue liners and connecting flue pipes (under development)
• prEN 13384-2 rev — thermal and fluid dynamic calculation methods Part 2 (stage 40.10, target Jan 2026)

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. No Art. 12 notification filed. Per COM(2025) 772 (First CPR Working Plan 2026–2029), a standardisation request covering most chimney and flue products is planned for adoption in Q3 2026. Self-standing chimneys (EN 13084 series) will be covered by a separate standardisation request for facade fire testing standards, planned for 2027. The CPR acquis sub-group for chimneys has not yet formally started but is expected to complete work in 2026. The future SReq will be issued under CPR 2024/3110 (new regulation), replacing the old mandate M/105.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2031–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q3 2026 and delivery expected 2028, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2031–2032. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 18 hENs and 11 EADs range from ~2031 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 227 + CEN/TC 336 + CEN/TC 154. 28 hENs.

info:
About this family: Covers products used in road, airfield, and trafficked-area construction, including asphalt and bituminous mixtures, paving-grade and polymer-modified bitumen, slurry surfacing, surface dressing, cold-applied joint sealants, road marking materials, concrete pavements, and hydraulically bound and unbound mixtures. The product scope is large, with around 25 harmonised standards. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 227, with CEN/TC 336 (bituminous binders) and CEN/TC 226 (road equipment) contributing related standards.

Current harmonised standards: ~25 harmonised standards cited in OJ under CPR 305/2011. AVCP per Commission Decision 98/601/EC (13 Oct 1998). Key series: EN 13108-1 to -9 (bituminous mixture specs), EN 13108-20/-21 (type testing/FPC), EN 12591 (paving bitumen), EN 14023 (polymer modified bitumen), EN 12273 (slurry surfacing), EN 12274 (surface dressing), EN 14188 (joint sealants), EN 1436/EN 1871 (road markings), EN 13877 (concrete pavements), EN 14227 (hydraulically bound mixtures), EN 13285 (unbound mixtures).

Standards in development: No CPR-mandated standard revisions currently under way. The CPR acquis process has not yet started for this family, so no standardisation-request-driven revisions are in the CEN/TC 227 pipeline.

Standardisation request: Not adopted. No draft on CIRCABC. Per COM(2025) 772 (First CPR Working Plan 2026–2029), the acquis process targets: Milestone I (scope) Q1 2026, Milestone III (essential characteristics) Q3 2026, standardisation request 2027. The future SReq will be under CPR 2024/3110 (new regulation). Road construction is in the later priority group), with acquis work not yet started.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2032–2033, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted 2027, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2032–2033. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 28 hENs range from ~2032 to ~2033 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

info:
About this family: One of the broadest product families, covering all types of floor coverings and paving used in buildings and external works. This includes resilient products (vinyl, linoleum, rubber, cork), textile and laminate floor coverings, wood flooring, ceramic tiles, natural stone tiles and slabs, concrete paving blocks, clay pavers, terrazzo, floor screeds, raised access floors, and indoor sports surfaces. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 134, with contributions from several related committees including TC 67 (ceramic tiles), TC 178 (paving units), TC 246 (natural stone), and TC 303 (floor screeds).

Current harmonised standards: ~19 hENs cited under CPR 305/2011 (OJ C 092/2018 and Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451). Key standards: EN 14041:2004/AC:2006 (resilient, textile and laminate — essential characteristics; EN 14041:2018 published by CEN but OJ citation unclear — likely standardisation crisis gap), EN 14342:2013 (wood flooring), EN 14411:2016 (ceramic tiles), EN 13813:2002 (screeds). AVCP Systems 1 (reaction to fire), 3 (slip resistance, formaldehyde, PCP), and 4 (other characteristics) per Commission Decision 97/808/EC. Plus EADs under EU 2019/450 with amendments through EU 2025/1769.

Standards development: EPLF (European Producers of Laminate Flooring) working on EN 14041 revision to integrate hazardous substances and environmental aspects into CE labelling. FEP (European Federation of Parquet Industry) active in adapting wood flooring standards to revised CPR. Milestone 0 work already ongoing on test methods for slip resistance — EN 16165:2021 (CEN/TC 339) published, Commission developing assessment method before formal acquis.

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. No acquis sub-group formally established. Per COM(2025) 772 Table 3: Milestone I targeted Q2 2026, Milestone III targeted Q4 2026, SReq targeted 2027. No standards delivery or mandatory dates set.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2031–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted 2027, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2031–2032. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 19 hENs range from ~2031 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + EOTA PA 34. EAD-only family — kits and systems.

info:
About this family: Covers an exceptionally diverse range of construction kits and prefabricated elements, from timber building kits and prefabricated building units to structural sandwich panels, earthquake-resistant steel-concrete kits, falling rock protection systems, and fibre-reinforced polymer strengthening kits. This family also includes family 36 (attached ladders) under the CPR Working Plan. Uniquely, products are assessed exclusively through European Assessment Documents and European Technical Assessments, with no harmonised EN standards. Standardisation is managed by EOTA, with around 32 active EADs in this product area.

EAD portfolio: ~32 active EADs cited in OJ under Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/450 with amendments through (EU) 2025/1769 (3 Sep 2025). 66 new EADs published across all product areas in Nov 2025 + 9 in Jan 2026. EOTA Stakeholder Conference (5 Nov 2025): "From Vision to Action: The New ETA Route" discussed the transition framework.

EAD transition under CPR 2024/3110: EADs under outgoing CPR can be used for issuing ETAs until 9 January 2031 (five years from entry into force). ETAs issued before that date remain valid for CE marking until 9 January 2036 (ten years from entry into force). New EADs must include environmental sustainability characteristics: GWP declaration immediately, core LCA-EPD indicators per EN 15804 by 2029, full indicator list by 2031. EADs now have 10-year validity with extension option. ETAs under the new CPR have no expiry date. Multiple entities (groups, associations, Commission) may now request ETAs — not just individual manufacturers.

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. No acquis sub-group formally established. Per COM(2025) 772 Table 3: Milestone I targeted Q2 2026, Milestone III targeted Q2 2027, SReq targeted 2027. No standards delivery or mandatory dates set. May be divided into several workstreams given the diversity of products. Commission may request EADs for innovative kits.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted 2027, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2032. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 32 EADs converge on ~2032. Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 128. 3 hENs (EN 12951, EN 516, EN 517) currently under ROC family 22, may be reclassified to family 36 under CPR 2024/3110. 0 EADs.

info:
About this family: This is a new product family introduced in CPR 2024/3110, covering permanently attached ladders fixed to buildings and other construction works. This includes fixed roof ladders, vertical access ladders on facades and building exteriors, caged and uncaged maintenance access ladders, and fixed ladders in utility shafts. Related roof access products such as walkways, treads and safety hooks are also in scope. Portable ladders, loft ladders, and machinery access ladders are excluded. No dedicated technical committee has been assigned; existing standards are under CEN/TC 128 (roof covering products).

Existing harmonised standards: Three standards currently cited under CPR 305/2011 in product family 22 (Roof Coverings) via Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451 — all AVCP System 3: (1) EN 12951:2004 — permanently fixed roof ladders on pitched roofs (closest existing standard to family 36 scope); (2) EN 516:2006 — roof access walkways, treads and steps; (3) EN 517:2006 — roof safety hooks and anchorage points. Under CPR 2024/3110, these may be reclassified from family 22 to family 36. Related standards under other legislation: EN ISO 14122-4:2016 (fixed ladders for machinery — Machinery Regulation), EN 353-1:2014+A1:2017 (guided fall arresters on fixed ladders — PPE Regulation).

Standards gap: EN 12951 covers only roof ladders on pitched roofs. Family 36's broader scope (facade ladders, utility access ladders, caged ladders) has no existing harmonised standards. New standards will need to be developed — likely via CEN mandate rather than EOTA EAD route, creating a potentially different pathway from the rest of family 34 (KAS).

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. No independent milestone data in Table 3 — family 36 is handled within family 34 (KAS) per COM(2025) 772. KAS milestones: M-I Q2 2026, M-III Q2 2027, SReq 2027. Table 3 notes "may be divided in several workstreams" — ladders could follow a separate timeline from KAS's main EOTA-based track.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2031–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: Based on the Commission Working Plan, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2031–2032. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 3 hENs range from ~2031 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + TC 459/SC 4 work programme. 0 current hENs (EN 10080 withdrawn). 2 prENs in development. 2 EADs for PT kits.

info:
About this family: Covers three distinct product groups: reinforcing steel (weldable bars, coils, and welded fabric for concrete reinforcement), prestressing steel (wire, strand, and bars), and post-tensioning kits (complete systems with tensile elements, anchorages, ducts, and filling material). Also includes stainless steel reinforcement. This is the only product family where the main harmonised standard was withdrawn, meaning reinforcing steel currently cannot be CE marked in the EU. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 459/SC 4.

Harmonised standards — unique situation: This is the only product family where the main harmonised standard was withdrawn from the OJ. EN 10080:2005 was withdrawn by Commission Decision 2006/893/EC because its "framework" approach left technical classes to national standards, making CE marking impossible. Since 2008, reinforcing steel cannot be CE marked in the EU. Prestressing steel (EN 10138 series) has been stuck at prEN stage for 20+ years and was never cited in the OJ. Only post-tensioning kits have a functioning CE marking route via ETAs under EAD 160004-00-0301 and EAD 160027-00-0301 (cited OJ C 435/07, 15 Dec 2017), AVCP System 1+. Major ETA holders include VSL, Freyssinet, DYWIDAG, OVM, and MK4.

Standards in development: prEN 10080:2023 — comprehensive revision (183 pages) went to CEN Enquiry in early 2023. Status post-enquiry unclear but likely being coordinated with the new SReq. prEN 10138-3:2023 (strand) at stage 40.60 (close of voting, Aug 2023). prEN 10138-2 (wire) at stage 20.60. prEN 10370 (stainless steel reinforcement) under development. Second-generation Eurocode 2 (EN 1992-1-1:2023) extends steel grades to B700 and adds stainless reinforcement — references EN 10080 and prEN 10138 despite neither functioning as a harmonised standard.

Standardisation request: Currently under preparation per COM(2025) 772 (one of 4 SReqs being prepared). CEN WP 2025 confirms CEN/TC 459/SC 4 "will participate in the consultation process around the draft Standardization Request." Old mandate M/115 (1997, revised 2009) is dormant. Assigned placeholder "M/XXX" — no mandate number yet. Target adoption Q2 2026. Delivery 2029, standard mandatory + DA 2029. Product requirements delegated act under discussion. CPR Acquis sub-group established Q4 2022 (M-I finished, M-III targeted Q4 2025). AVCP System 1+ (Decision 97/597/EC). European Ombudsman case opened Jan 2019 on Commission's failure to follow up on M/115.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q2 2026 and delivery expected 2029, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2030–2031. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 2 hENs and 2 EADs range from ~2030 to ~2032. The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2030–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2030–2031).

summary.source: Draft SReq (Jan 2026) + OJ C 092/2018 + EOTA PA 2. 15 hENs + 7 EADs.

info:
About this family: One of the broadest and most commercially significant product families, covering windows, external and internal pedestrian doorsets, industrial and garage doors, fire and smoke control doorsets, powered pedestrian doors, external blinds, awnings, shutters, roof windows, and hatches. It also includes building hardware such as door closers, hold-open devices, emergency and panic exit devices, hinges, and locks. With around 50,000 manufacturers and an estimated 76 million DPPs per year for windows alone, this family represents a major implementation challenge. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 33.

Current harmonised standards (~15 hENs + ~7 EADs): EN 14351-1:2006+A2:2016 (windows/external doors, AVCP System 1 for fire / System 3 for thermal/acoustic), EN 13241:2003+A2:2016 (industrial/commercial/garage doors, also under Machinery Directive), EN 16034:2014 (fire/smoke — must be used with EN 14351-1 or EN 13241), EN 13561:2015 and EN 13659:2015 (blinds/shutters, System 4), and 8 building hardware standards (EN 1154, EN 1155, EN 1158, EN 179, EN 1125, EN 1935, EN 12209, EN 14846 — all System 1 for escape/fire, System 3 otherwise). All cited via OJ C 092/6 and Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451 under old CPR 305/2011 mandate M/101. EN 14351-2 (internal doors) published 2018 but NEVER cited — victim of the post-James Elliott standardisation deficit where the Commission froze new hEN citations.

Standards in development: The draft SReq (21 Jan 2026) requests 15 deliverables: 4 new product standards (windows/hatches/doorsets, industrial/commercial/garage doors, rooflights/hatches, building hardware — all due 15 Jun 2029), 5 new EPD product category rules (due Jun–Dec 2028), and 6 revisions (EN 13241 gates/barriers due Jun 2029; EN 13561 awnings, EN 13561 external blinds, EN 13120 internal blinds, EN 13659 shutters — all due 15 Dec 2029; EN 17610 EPD PCR building hardware due Dec 2028). prEN 14351-3 also exists at CEN — would merge EN 16034's fire/smoke approach into the EN 14351 series. Dangerous substances assessment will follow the horizontal SReq for dangerous substances (1 Sep 2025, under CPR 2024/3110, 15 deliverables by CEN/TC 351, all due 31 Dec 2029 — covers PFAS, microplastics, VVOC, asbestos, radioactivity).

Standardisation request: Draft Art. 12 notification published 21 Jan 2026 via the EC Notification System. Feedback deadline was 18 Feb 2026. NOT yet adopted — EuroWindoor submitted comments 13 Feb 2026. Under CPR 2024/3110. Replaces mandate M/101 (expires 31 Dec 2026 per Article 5). Decision expires 30 Jun 2031. First annual report due 30 Jun 2027. Final report due 30 Jun 2030. Scope: 51 technical characteristics + 19 environmental criteria + 257 substances for windows alone. Industry pushback: 86 comments on 4th draft (Oct 2025), EuroWindoor calls timeline "highly ambitious", wants 1-year extension. AVCP System 1 for fire/escape; System 3 otherwise (Decision 1999/93/EC, amended 2011/246/EU). Joint AVS 4+ proposal (Feb 2026) for verified software assessment. Product requirements DA for escape routes targeted 2029. Acquis sub-group established Q4 2022 (M-I and M-III both finished).

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request draft published and delivery expected Jun 2029, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2030–2031. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 15 hENs and 7 EADs range from ~2030 to ~2032 (13 of 15 hENs under CPR 305/2011, needing revision under CPR 2024/3110). The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2030–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2030–2031).

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 88 + DIBt hEN list + EOTA PA 4. 12 hENs + 18 EADs.

info:
About this family: The largest insulation product family under the CPR, covering factory-made thermal insulation products across 13 material types including mineral wool, expanded and extruded polystyrene, rigid polyurethane, phenolic foam, cellular glass, wood wool, wood fibre, expanded cork, expanded perlite, polyethylene, and calcium silicate. Also includes external thermal insulation composite systems (ETICS) assessed via the EAD route, vacuum insulation panels, and reflective insulation. This is a substantial market, valued at approximately USD 21 billion globally. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 88.

Current harmonised standards: 13 hENs for factory-made products: EN 13162–13171 series (one per insulation type) plus EN 14063-1, EN 16069, and EN 16977. All cited under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/103 (last OJ citation: OJ C 092, 2018 / Implementing Decision 2019/451). Important correction: EN 13499 and EN 13500 (ETICS with EPS/mineral wool) are not cited in the Official Journal and never functioned as true harmonised standards — ETICS products use the EAD/ETA route instead. AVCP: System 3/4 for factory-made products (Decision 99/91/EC); System 1 for ETICS (Decision 97/556/EC, amended by 2011/14/EU).

ETICS dual pathway: ETICS kits currently rely exclusively on the EAD/ETA route. ETAG 004 was fully converted to EAD 040083-01-0404 in 2025, with approximately 798 valid ETAs. A future harmonised product standard — EN 17237 — is in development by CEN/TC 88/WG 18 under mandate M/489, but is estimated to be 7+ years from completion. No harmonised facade fire test exists at EU level — national divergence persists (BS 8414 UK, DIN 4102-20 DE, LEPIR 2 FR). The European Association for ETICS (EAE) represents >80% of Europe's ETICS revenue. Around 15–20 active EADs cover innovative products including VIPs, aerogels, and bio-based insulation.

Standardisation request: In preparation — targeted Q3 2026 per COM(2025) 772 Table 3. Not yet notified under Art. 12 (as of Feb 2026). Acquis Milestone I (scope definition) finished; Milestone III (technical content for SReq + delegated acts) ongoing. Product requirements delegated act under discussion. The SReq will be under CPR 2024/3110 (new CPR), meaning DPP obligations apply directly once the resulting HTS is in force. Separately, the horizontal dangerous substances SReq (CEN/TC 351) was notified 1 Sep 2025 and covers all families including TIP — key substances: HBCDD, formaldehyde, biocides, mineral fibres. M/103 (mid-1990s) will be superseded by the new SReq.

Environmental & sustainability: EN 16783:2024 (EPD Product Category Rules for insulation) published. GWP declaration becomes mandatory from January 2026 under CPR 2024/3110. AVS delegated act circulated to Member States July 2025. ESPR: thermal insulation is not on the first priority product list (2025–2030 working plan) — CPR 2024/3110 is the primary instrument for environmental performance requirements. No dual ESPR/CPR DPP obligation expected in the near term (ESPR mid-term review in 2028 could add construction products).

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2029–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q3 2026 and delivery expected 2029, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2029–2030. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 12 hENs and 18 EADs range from ~2031 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the policy pipeline’s earlier estimate (~2029–2030) to the standards-based assessment’s later horizon (~2031–2032).

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 129 + EOTA PA 30. 12 hENs + 4 EADs.

info:
About this family: Covers all types of flat glass, profiled glass, and glass blocks used in buildings — from basic float glass and toughened safety glass to laminated glass, insulating glass units, coated glass, and specialty products like borosilicate and chemically strengthened glass. Six European Assessment Documents also cover niche innovations such as vacuum-insulated glass units and thermally curved glass. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 129 (Glass in building), which coordinates closely with Glass for Europe.

Current harmonised standards: ~12 standard families cited under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/135 (OJ C 092/2018 and Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451). Key hENs: EN 572 series (basic glass), EN 1096 series (coated glass), EN 1279 series (insulating glass units), EN 12150-2 (toughened safety glass), EN 1863-2 (heat-strengthened), EN 14449 (laminated glass), EN 1051-2 (glass blocks), EN 1748-1-2/1748-2-2 (borosilicate/glass-ceramic), EN 14178-2 (alkaline earth silicate), EN 12337-2 (chemically strengthened), EN 14179-2 (heat-soaked toughened).

AVCP system: Commission Decision 2000/245/EC — System 1 (fire resistance, anti-bullet, anti-explosion glass); System 3 (other safety glass — toughened, laminated without fire/security function); System 4 (regular glass products). Basic glass classified A1 (non-combustible) without testing per Decision 96/603/EC. CEN/TC 129 recommends NPD for reaction to fire of laminated glass and IGUs (SBI test not appropriate for glass products).

Standardisation request: Acquis Milestone I (product scope) finished; Milestone III (essential characteristics) ongoing. SReq GLA targeted Q1 2026 per COM(2025) 772 Table 3. Under CPR 2024/3110 (DPP applies directly). No Art. 12 notification found as of Feb 2026 — Q1 2026 target at risk. Glass for Europe confirms 30+ product standards to be written or revised. SReq aligned with windows/doors SReq timeline (CEN/TC 33). Product requirements DA (Article 7 — minimum performance thresholds) identified for 2028. IGU alignment gap: window hEN target Dec 2028 but CEN/TC 129 IGU standards not until Dec 2030 — 2-year component mismatch flagged by industry.

Environmental & sustainability: EN 17074:2019 is the dedicated EPD PCR for flat glass (cradle to gate/grave). Glass for Europe opposes worst-case approach for environmental declarations — complex multi-site value chain makes this impractical. EPBD guidance (June 2025) pushes U-value + g-value for transparent building elements. Only 5% of construction flat glass is recycled; industry calls for 15% minimum recycled content target. ESPR: glass NOT on 2025-2030 priority list. Also affected by horizontal dangerous substances SReq (CEN/TC 351, all due 31 Dec 2029).

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2029–2032, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q1 2026 and delivery expected 2028, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2029–2030. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 12 hENs and 4 EADs range from ~2029 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2029–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2029–2030).

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 124 + CEN/TC 112 + EOTA PA 13.

info:
About this family: Covers structural timber products including strength-graded solid timber, glued laminated timber (glulam), cross-laminated timber (CLT), laminated veneer lumber (LVL), finger-jointed timber, prefabricated trusses, wood poles, and timber fasteners and connectors. Around 41 European Assessment Documents also cover innovative products such as hardwood glulam, wood-concrete composites, and laminated strand lumber. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 124 (Timber structures), with key industry bodies including CEI-Bois and EOS.

Current harmonised standards: ~9 harmonised standards cited under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/112 (OJ C 092/2018 and Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451). Key hENs: EN 14081-1:2016+A1:2019 (strength graded timber — System 2+), EN 14080:2013 (glulam — System 1), EN 16351:2021 (CLT — System 1), EN 14374:2004 (LVL — System 1), EN 15497:2014 (finger-jointed — System 1), EN 14250:2010 (prefab trusses — System 1/2+), EN 14229:2010 (wood poles — System 2+), EN 14545:2008 (connectors — System 2+/3), EN 14592:2022 (dowel fasteners — System 3). EN 16351 is a key success story — CLT previously relied on EAD 130005-00-0304; harmonised standard published 2015, revised 2021. Delegated Regulations (EU) 2017/2293 and (EU) 2017/1227 provide reaction-to-fire classification without testing for CLT, LVL, glulam, and finger-jointed timber.

AVCP system: Commission Decision 97/176/EC — System 1 (structural glued products: glulam, LVL, finger-jointed, CLT, load-bearing trusses — certification body required); System 2+ (strength-graded solid timber, wood poles — FPC surveillance by notified body); System 3 (dowel-type fasteners, some connectors — third-party testing). Fire classification: untreated timber typically D-s2,d0; fire-retardant treatment can achieve B-s1,d0. Eurocode 5 (EN 1995) uses charring rate approach for fire design (softwood: 0.65 mm/min one-dimensional). Second-generation EN 1995-1-1:2025 substantially expanded for CLT and LVL — distribution to NSBs by Mar 2026.

Standardisation request: SReq STP targeted Q3 2026 per COM(2025) 772 Table 3. Under CPR 2024/3110 (DPP applies directly). No Art. 12 notification found as of Feb 2026 — consistent with Q3 2026 target. Will require revision of all ~9 existing hENs plus new standards. Milestone I ongoing, Milestone III targeted Q1 2026. Also affected by horizontal dangerous substances SReq (CEN/TC 351, notified 1 Sep 2025 — timber-specific concerns include preservatives, formaldehyde, biocides, VOCs).

Environmental & sustainability: EN 16485 is the dedicated EPD PCR for wood/wood-based products (supplements EN 15804+A2). Biogenic carbon accounting under EN 15804+A2 uses the -1/+1 method: carbon uptake credited in modules A1-A3, release assumed at end-of-life in C3/C4 — timber industry argues this undervalues temporary carbon storage during 50-100 year building service life. EUDR (EU Deforestation Regulation) enforcement from Dec 2026 creates parallel supply chain traceability requirements with DPP synergy potential. FSC/PEFC chain-of-custody provides pre-existing digital infrastructure. France RE2020 gives biogenic carbon bonus + 50% timber mandate for public buildings. Nordic countries imposing whole-building carbon limits (Denmark 2023, Sweden/Finland 2025). UK combustibles ban for residential >18m external walls. Germany MBO: timber to 5 storeys, MHolzBauRL updated Sep 2024. ESPR: timber NOT on 2025-2030 priority list.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2029–2033, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q3 2026 and delivery expected 2029, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2029–2030. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 9 hENs and 41 EADs range from ~2032 to ~2033 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the policy pipeline’s earlier estimate (~2029–2030) to the standards-based assessment’s later horizon (~2032–2033).

info:
About this family: One of the broadest product families, spanning electronic fire detection and alarm systems, fixed sprinkler and gas extinguishing components, smoke and heat control systems, fire dampers, fire hydrants, hose reels, residential sprinklers, and explosion suppression equipment. Four European Assessment Documents cover specialty products. Standardisation is shared across multiple technical committees: CEN/TC 191 (fixed firefighting), CEN/TC 72 (fire detection and alarm), CEN/TC 295 (residential sprinklers), and CEN/TC 305 (explosion suppression), with Euralarm as the key industry body.

Current harmonised standards: ~32 harmonised standards cited under old CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/109 (OJ C 092/2018, Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451). AVCP per Decision 96/577/EC: System 1 for ALL products (most stringent — NB certification required). EN 54 citation backlog: 6+ technically sound standards remain uncited since ~2014 due to James Elliott ruling fallout. Euralarm notes CPR covers "asphalt to electronic systems" under one framework — fire safety needs faster standards update cycles than the current process delivers.

Standardisation request: Acquis NOT started. M-I targeted Q2 2026, M-III Q1 2027, SReq Q3 2027 per COM(2025) 772 Table 3. No Art. 12 notification found. Under CPR 2024/3110 (DPP applies directly). No standards delivery date set — the breadth of this family (dual TC, ~32 existing hENs) means development will take 3-4 years minimum. EFSN confirms CEN/TC 191/WG 5 has NOT begun revising sprinkler hENs — waiting for the SReq.

Multi-directive & environmental: Fire detection products uniquely fall under CPR + EMC (2014/30/EU) + LVD (2014/35/EU) + RoHS (2011/65/EU) + WEEE (2012/19/EU) + Cyber Resilience Act (from Dec 2027). No fire-safety-specific EPD PCR exists — active systems have unique LCA challenges (module B6 operational energy over 15-25 year lifespan, electronic end-of-life). Apollo Fire Detectors targeting EPDs by 2030 using CIBSE TM65 as interim. ESPR: fire safety NOT on priority list but horizontal EEE repairability measures (~2027) could capture electronic detection products.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2033, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q3 2027, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2032–2033. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 33 hENs and 4 EADs range from ~2030 to ~2032 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the standards-based assessment’s earlier estimate (~2030–2032) to the policy pipeline’s later horizon (~2032–2033).

info:
About this family: One of the most fragmented product families, covering internal and external wall cladding, ceiling finishes, and internal partition kits. Products range from fibre-cement sheets, metal cladding, and sandwich panels to suspended ceilings, decorative wallcoverings, natural stone cladding, wood panelling, and high-pressure laminates. Standardisation is spread across seven or more technical committees, primarily CEN/TC 128 (roof and wall cladding), with secondary roles for CEN/TC 277 (suspended ceilings), CEN/TC 241 (gypsum), CEN/TC 99 (wallcoverings), CEN/TC 246 (natural stone), CEN/TC 175 (wood panelling), and CEN/TC 134 (high-pressure laminates).

Current harmonised standards & AVCP: ~22 hENs cited under CPR 305/2011 via mandate M/121 (OJ C 092/2018, Implementing Decision (EU) 2019/451). AVCP per Commission Decision 98/437/EC (amended by 2001/596/EC): System 1 for reaction-to-fire classes A1–C (NB certification), System 3 for fire classes D–F and fire protection panels, System 4 for other uses (acoustic, thermal, moisture). Significant scope overlap with PF 22 (roof coverings — many standards dual-listed), PF 7 (gypsum — EN 14195/EN 13950), PF 19 (floorings — EN 14411 ceramics), PF 20 (structural metallic — EN 14509 sandwich panels). Note: wallpaper (EN 15102/CEN/TC 99) may be separated into a new family "Decorative paints and wallpapers" under CPR 2024/3110. No EADs in product area 21.

Fire safety & post-Grenfell: Reaction to fire is the dominant performance characteristic — drives AVCP system assignment. New EU facade fire test standard effective Jan 2026 (temperature-based measurement replacing visual assessment, 7 years in development). Post-Grenfell (UK Phase 2 report Sep 2024): UK extending combustible materials ban to buildings >11m (Sep 2025, Euroclass A2 minimum for external facades). 14 EU member states have national fire deviations beyond the Euroclass system. UK Construction Products Reform Green Paper (Feb 2025) proposes a "Construction Library" central repository — proto-DPP concept.

info:
About this family: Covers all gypsum-based construction products including plasterboards, fibreboards, gypsum blocks, plasters, fibrous casts, composite panels, reprocessed gypsum products, metal framing for drylining systems, and ceiling elements. Two European Assessment Documents address structural/load-bearing plasterboard. A complete drylining or partition system involves boards, framing, fasteners, jointing, and adhesives, each with separate standards. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 241 (Gypsum products).

AVCP & fire classification: One of the lightest AVCP regimes among all 37 product families. Per Commission Decision 95/467/EC (amended 2002/592/EC): System 3/4 for most products — manufacturer self-declaration. System 1 only for fire-critical products (composite panels, fibrous casts, jointing, metal framing). Gypsum (mineral) classified A1 without testing per Decision 96/603/EC — inherent fire safety guaranteed by material composition (paper liner ≤220 g/m² → A2-s1,d0 per EN 520 Annex B). This is a key competitive advantage: fire performance is a selling point rather than a compliance burden.

Circularity & sustainability: Gypsum is 100% recyclable with no property loss, yet only ~13% post-consumer recycled (87% to landfill). Unique challenge: gypsum in mixed landfill produces toxic H₂S gas (UK banned co-disposal 2005). FGD synthetic gypsum declining as Europe decarbonises — urgency to increase C&D recycling. Siniat RECYPLAC: world's first 100% recycled gypsum plasterboard (Jul 2025). EU LIFE GtoG project demonstrated 30% recycled content. Industry: Eurogypsum (EUR 7.7B turnover, 154 quarries, 160 factories). Three European producers — Knauf, Saint-Gobain, Etex — control ~47% of global wallboard capacity. EPDs well-established (IBU Part B PCR for plasterboard). BIM-ready: Knauf Plug-in 2025, Saint-Gobain BIM portal.

info:
About this family: One of the most compact CPR families, covering structural bearings used in bridges and buildings — including sliding, elastomeric, roller, pot, rocker, spherical PTFE, and guide/restraint bearings — plus pins for structural joints. Six European Assessment Documents address innovative bearing materials, reflecting material innovation outpacing the standard revision cycle. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 167 (Structural bearings). Anti-seismic devices are a separate product family.

AVCP & Eurocode connection: Per Decision 95/467/EC: System 1 for critical bearings (failure consequences beyond limit states — most bridge bearings), System 3 for non-critical. Second-generation Eurocodes deepening integration: EN 1990 adding new Annex F specifically for bearing design (migrating from EN 1993-2 informative Annex A). EN 1998-2 (seismic bridges) references both EN 1337 and EN 15129 — manufacturers must navigate two TCs and two AVCP regimes.

Industry & infrastructure: Oligopolistic niche — no dedicated European trade association. Key manufacturers: mageba (CH), Maurer (DE), FIP MEC (IT), Freyssinet/VINCI (FR), Trelleborg (SE). DIBt (Germany) is the leading TAB. Market ~USD 180M (Europe). Infrastructure-driven: 200,000+ railway bridges in EU, most >50 years old. Bearings last 30–50 years vs bridge lifespan 75–120 years → replaced 1–3 times per bridge. Demand weighted toward replacement/maintenance. EPD at ground zero: first externally verified EPD published Dec 2024 (mageba RESTON SPHERICAL, EPD Hub). No bearing-specific PCR exists.

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. Per COM(2025) 772 Table 3: Milestone I (product scope) targeted Q4 2026, Milestone III (essential characteristics) targeted Q4 2027, standardisation request targeted 2028. info:
About this family: One of the richest product families, covering roof coverings (clay tiles, concrete tiles, fibre-cement slates, bitumen sheets, natural slate, metal sheets), roof lights, roof windows, rigid underlays, polycarbonate sheets, sandwich panels, and permanent roof safety anchors. With 32 European Assessment Documents it has the second-highest EAD count across all 37 families, including green roof kits and recycled plastic roofing. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 128 (Roof and wall cladding).

AVCP & fire: Commission Decision 98/436/EC (amended 2001/596/EC) — System 1 for reaction to fire classes A1–C; System 3 for fire classes D–F, durability, water tightness; System 4 for remaining characteristics. Separate Decision 2000/553/EC governs external fire performance of roof coverings (BROOF classification — a characteristic unique to roofing). EN 13501-5 classifies external fire exposure to roofs. Fire is the dominant driver of AVCP complexity: most product standards carry triple systems (1/3/4) depending on the declared fire class.

BIPV & green roofs: Building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) — solar roof tiles/panels that serve both energy generation and building envelope functions — straddle CPR and ESPR (Ecodesign). No harmonised BIPV standard exists under CPR; CE marking for BIPV as a construction product is not yet possible. Table 3 notes "PV panels regulated by Ecodesign/ESPR, work aligned." Two green roof EADs (220009 and 220078) cover kits with substrate/drainage/vegetation layers — a growing market segment with no hEN route. 32 EADs also include recycled plastic roofing (220010, 220069), sun tunnels (220021), fire-resistant roof windows (220116), and roof windows with integrated balconies (220150).

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. Per COM(2025) 772 Table 3: Milestone I (product scope) targeted 2027, Milestone III (essential characteristics) targeted 2027, standardisation request targeted 2028. Eleven European Assessment Documents extend the scope into bridge expansion joints and specialty products. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 226 (Road equipment), which also has a newer working group on road infrastructure interactions with autonomous vehicles.

AVCP & safety: Commission Decision 96/579/EC — System 1 for 80% of standards (12 of 15), reflecting the safety-critical nature of road equipment. Only anti-glare screens and noise barriers carry System 3. No System 4 at all. EN 1317 has 8 parts but only Part 5 is harmonised; Parts 4/7 are under revision (splitting terminal/transition standards), Part 6 is a Technical Report (pedestrian parapets), Part 8 is a Technical Specification (motorcycle restraints). EN 1317-5 demands full-scale vehicle crash tests at speeds up to 110 km/h — one of the most physically demanding test regimes in all of CPR. Milestone 0 already ongoing for a new road restraint assessment method — a unique pre-acquis step where the Commission is developing methodology before formal standardisation begins. Environmental Noise Directive 2002/49/EC creates a parallel demand driver for EN 14388 noise barriers through mandatory noise action plans.

Bridge joints & multi-directive: 7 of 11 EADs cover bridge expansion joints (EAD 120011 flexible plug, 120093 asphaltic plug, 120109 nosing, 120110 mat, 120111 cantilever, 120112 supported, 120113 modular) — the family extends well beyond road-surface equipment into bridge infrastructure, overlapping with SBE (structural bearings). Other EADs: microprismatic retroreflective sheeting (120001), wood/metal lighting columns (120003/120017), road marking products (230011). Variable message signs (EN 12966) face multi-directive complexity similar to FFF: EMC Directive 2014/30/EU, Low Voltage Directive 2014/35/EU, and potentially Cyber Resilience Act for connected/IoT signage. The IRMA working group on automated mobility signals future standards for V2I (vehicle-to-infrastructure) road equipment — a growth area for intelligent road infrastructure.

Standardisation request: Not yet adopted. Per COM(2025) 772 Table 3: Milestone I (product scope) targeted 2027, Milestone III (essential characteristics) targeted 2027, standardisation request targeted 2028. No standards delivery or mandatory adoption dates set yet. The future SReq will be issued under CPR 2024/3110.

Both scenarios converge on the same timeline.

summary.source: COM(2025) 772 + CEN/TC 104 + CEN/TC 125 + CEN/TC 303 + CEN/TC 67 + EOTA PA 26.

info:
About this family: Covers concrete admixtures, mineral additions (fly ash, blast furnace slag, silica fume), fibres (steel and polymer), pigments, and the full range of concrete repair products — but notably excludes ready-mixed concrete itself, since the core concrete specification is not harmonised under CPR. Nineteen European Assessment Documents represent the frontier of low-carbon concrete innovation, including steel fibres from end-of-life tyres, FRP reinforcement bars, and calcined clay for low-carbon cements. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 104 (Concrete and related products), with a close relationship to CEN/TC 51 (Cement).

AVCP & complexity: Commission Decision 1999/469/EC — the most AVCP-complex family: EN 1504 repair products carry up to 4 different systems (1, 2+, 3, 4) depending on the characteristic. System 1+ for mineral additions (fly ash, slag, silica fume — safety-critical as they modify concrete strength). System 2+ for admixtures (EN 934 series) and pigments. System 1/3 for fibres (structural vs non-structural use). System 4 only for non-structural surface protection. The acquis work (Milestone I: ongoing, Milestone III: Q1 2026) could bring EN 206 into CPR scope for the first time — potentially transformational for the ~EUR 80B European ready-mixed concrete market.

Circularity & innovation: 19 EADs show strong circular economy innovation: EAD 260010 (steel fibres recovered from end-of-life tyres), EAD 260009 (MSWI bottom ash as concrete addition), EAD 260023 (carbon/glass/basalt/aramid FRP reinforcement bars — alternative to steel), EAD 260014 (calcined clay as type II addition — key for LC3 low-carbon cements), EAD 260020 (catalytic calcium carbonate), EAD 260006 (polymeric addition), EAD 260035 (natural calcined pozzolana). Also includes FRP structural sections (260001), inorganic fibre grid reinforcement (260057), and zinc anode cathodic protection systems (260100). Many of these EADs represent the frontier of low-carbon concrete technology.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is ~2030–2033, informed by two scenarios. Policy pipeline: With standardisation request targeted Q4 2026 and delivery expected 2029, the regulatory pathway points to DPP by ~2030–2031. Standards-based: Per-standard DPP estimates across 16 hENs and 19 EADs range from ~2032 to ~2033 (all hENs currently cited under CPR 305/2011, requiring revision under CPR 2024/3110 before DPP applies). The combined range spans from the policy pipeline’s earlier estimate (~2030–2031) to the standards-based assessment’s later horizon (~2032–2033).

About this family: Covers interior and exterior decorative paints, varnishes, coatings, and wallcoverings for buildings. This is a new product family introduced under the revised CPR. Standardisation is led by CEN/TC 139 (Paints and varnishes).

Standardisation request: According to the Commission Working Plan (Table 3), Milestone I (product scope) is planned for 2029. No date for Milestone III (essential characteristics) has been set, and no standardisation request is expected for several years. As a newly added family with no existing acquis, the entire standardisation process must be built from scratch.

DPP outlook: The estimated DPP range is 2035+. Policy pipeline: Based on the Commission Working Plan, DPP is projected by 2035+. No individual standard data is available to provide an alternative scenario.
